figures.caption: Figure 1: (a) Average search count per trajectory increases over training under answercorrectness reward alone. (b) The within-batch depth distribution shifts toward higher values as training advances.

Table 1: Main results under ZeroSearch (simulator evaluation) and Search-R1 frameworks (EM, %). GRPO-Full and GRPO-Half correspond to the standard training protocol of each framework without trajectory selection. All SDGA variants use Smax = 5. Bold denotes the best result per model group.
Table 4: Performance under different Smax settings (Qwen2.5-3B, ZeroSearch simulator evaluation, EM, %).

Table 3: Ablation on trajectory selection signals under the ZeroSearch framework (simulator evaluation, Qwen2.5-3B, EM, %). Random and Top-K Reward share the same rollout generation and update cost as CuSearch.
Figure 4: Training template. The question is appended to the end during training and inference.

Figure 3: Training dynamics on ZeroSearch with Qwen2.5-3B. Panels (a–c) show EM on NQ, mean reward, and average search depth per rollout. SDGA-Phase achieves the highest values on all three metrics by the end of training.

Figure 2: Overview of CuSearch. Top: A query generates N ·G rollouts. SDGA then selects K by search depth for gradient updates. Bottom left: SDGA greedily allocates budget K across depth buckets by priority and capacity. Bottom right: SDGA-Auto always targets the deepest available bucket, with selections advancing deeper as the depth distribution shifts upward during training. SDGA-Phase explicitly advances the target bucket as deeper trajectories become sufficiently abundant.
